sourceQuestion:
An array X of n distinct integers is interpreted as a complete binary tree. The index of the first element of the array is 0. If the root node's index is R in the array, and if the only element of the array that is the root node satisfies the heap property, then the algorithm to convert the complete binary tree into a heap has time complexity

A. O(log n)
B. O(n)
C. O(n log n)
D. O(n^2 log n)

parsed.stem: An array X of n distinct integers is interpreted as a complete binary tree. The index of the first element of the array is 0. If the root node's index is R in the array, and if the only element of the array that is the root node satisfies the heap property, then the algorithm to convert the complete binary tree into a heap has time complexity

sourceQuestion:
A binary search tree is generated by inserting in order the following integers:
50, 15, 62, 5, 20, 58, 91, 3, 8, 37, 60, 24
The number of nodes in the left subtree and right subtree of the root respectively is
A. (4, 7)
B. (7, 4)
C. (8, 3)
D. (3, 1)

parsed.stem:
A binary search tree is generated by inserting in order the following integers:
50, 15, 62, 5, 20, 58, 91, 3, 8, 37, 60, 24
The number of nodes in the left subtree and right subtree of the root respectively is

sourceQuestion:
A binary search tree contains the values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8. The tree is traversed in pre-order and the values are printed out. Which of the following sequences is a valid pre-order output?
A. 5 3 1 2 4 7 8 6
B. 5 3 1 2 4 7 6 8
C. 5 3 2 4 1 6 7 8
D. 5 3 1 2 4 6 8 7

parsed.stem: A binary search tree contains the values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8. The tree is traversed in pre-order and the values are printed out. Which of the following sequences is a valid pre-order output?

sourceQuestion:
Suppose the numbers 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9, 4 are inserted in that order into an initially empty binary search tree. The binary search tree uses the usual ordering on natural numbers. What is the in-order traversal sequence of the resultant tree?
A. 7 5 1 0 3 2 4 6 8 9
B. 0 2 4 3 1 6 5 9 8 7
C. 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 9
D. 9 8 6 4 2 3 0 1 5 7

parsed.stem: Suppose the numbers 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9, 4 are inserted in that order into an initially empty binary search tree. The binary search tree uses the usual ordering on natural numbers. What is the in-order traversal sequence of the resultant tree?

sourceQuestion:
Let T(n) be the number of different binary search trees on n distinct elements. Then T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1) * T(n-k), where T(0) = 1. Which of the following is T(n)?
A. n = 4k + 1
B. n = k
C. n = k - 1
D. n = k - 2

parsed.stem: Let T(n) be the number of different binary search trees on n distinct elements. Then T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1) * T(n-k), where T(0) = 1. Which of the following is T(n)?

sourceQuestion:
A data structure is required for storing a set of integers such that each of the following operations can be done in O(log n) time, where n is the number of elements in the set:
I. Deletion of the smallest element
II. Insertion of an element if it is not already present in the set
Which of the following data structures can be used for this purpose?
A. A heap can be used but not a balanced binary search tree
B. A balanced binary search tree can be used but not a heap
C. Both balanced binary search tree and heap can be used
D. Neither balanced binary search tree nor heap can be used
sourceAnswer: B. A balanced binary search tree can be used but not a heap

parsed.stem:
A data structure is required for storing a set of integers such that each of the following operations can be done in O(log n) time, where n is the number of elements in the set:
I. Deletion of the smallest element
II. Insertion of an element if it is not already present in the set
Which of the following data structures can be used for this purpose?

options.text: A heap can be used but not a balanced binary search tree
A balanced binary search tree can be used but not a heap
Both balanced binary search tree and heap can be used
Neither balanced binary search tree nor heap can be used

sourceQuestion:
The following numbers are inserted into an empty binary search tree in the given order: 10, 1, 3, 5, 15, 12, 16. What is the height of the binary search tree (the height is the maximum distance of a leaf node from the root)?
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 6

parsed.stem: The following numbers are inserted into an empty binary search tree in the given order: 10, 1, 3, 5, 15, 12, 16. What is the height of the binary search tree (the height is the maximum distance of a leaf node from the root)?

sourceQuestion:
A program takes as input a balanced binary search tree with n leaf nodes and computes the value of a function g(x) for each node x. If the cost of computing g(x) is min(number of leaf-nodes in left-subtree of x, number of leaf-nodes in right-subtree of x), then the worst-case time complexity of the program is?
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(n^2)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. Theta(n^2 log n)

parsed.stem: A program takes as input a balanced binary search tree with n leaf nodes and computes the value of a function g(x) for each node x. If the cost of computing g(x) is min(number of leaf-nodes in left-subtree of x, number of leaf-nodes in right-subtree of x), then the worst-case time complexity of the program is?

sourceQuestion:
Postorder traversal of a given binary search tree T produces the following sequence of keys: 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29. Which one of the following sequences of keys can be the result of an in-order traversal of the tree T?
A. 9, 3, 10, 25, 27, 29
B. 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29
C. 3, 10, 9, 25, 29, 27
D. 9, 10, 25, 27, 29, 3

parsed.stem: Postorder traversal of a given binary search tree T produces the following sequence of keys: 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29. Which one of the following sequences of keys can be the result of an in-order traversal of the tree T?

sourceQuestion:
How many distinct binary search trees can be created out of 4 distinct keys?
A. 5
B. 14
C. 24
D. 42

parsed.stem: How many distinct binary search trees can be created out of 4 distinct keys?

sourceQuestion:
You are given the postorder traversal, P, of a binary search tree on the elements {1, 2, ..., n}. You have to determine the unique binary search tree that has P as its postorder traversal. What is the time complexity of the most efficient algorithm for doing this?
A. Theta(log n)
B. Theta(n)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. None of the above, as the tree cannot be uniquely determined

parsed.stem: You are given the postorder traversal, P, of a binary search tree on the elements {1, 2, ..., n}. You have to determine the unique binary search tree that has P as its postorder traversal. What is the time complexity of the most efficient algorithm for doing this?

options.text: None of the above, as the tree cannot be uniquely determined

sourceQuestion:
The worst case running time to search for an element in a balanced binary search tree with n^2 elements is
A. Theta(n^2 log n)
B. Theta(n^2)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. O(log n)

parsed.stem: The worst case running time to search for an element in a balanced binary search tree with n^2 elements is

sourceQuestion:
The preorder traversal sequence of a binary search tree is 30, 20, 10, 15, 25, 23, 39, 35, 42. Which one of the following is the postorder traversal sequence of the same tree?
A. 10, 15, 25, 23, 20, 35, 42, 39, 30
B. 15, 10, 23, 25, 20, 42, 35, 39, 30
C. 15, 20, 10, 23, 25, 39, 30, 42, 35
D. 15, 10, 25, 23, 20, 42, 35, 39, 30

parsed.stem: The preorder traversal sequence of a binary search tree is 30, 20, 10, 15, 25, 23, 39, 35, 42. Which one of the following is the postorder traversal sequence of the same tree?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following is the tightest upper bound that represents the time complexity of inserting an object into a binary search tree of n nodes?
A. O(1)
B. O(log n)
C. O(n)
D. O(n log n)

parsed.stem: Which one of the following is the tightest upper bound that represents the time complexity of inserting an object into a binary search tree of n nodes?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is/are correct inorder traversal sequence(s) of binary search tree(s)?
I. 3, 5, 7, 8, 15, 19, 25
II. 5, 8, 9, 12, 10, 15, 25
III. 2, 7, 10, 8, 14, 16, 20
IV. 4, 6, 7, 9, 18, 20, 25
A. I and IV only
B. II and III only
C. II and IV only
D. II only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following is/are correct inorder traversal sequence(s) of binary search tree(s)?
I. 3, 5, 7, 8, 15, 19, 25
II. 5, 8, 9, 12, 10, 15, 25
III. 2, 7, 10, 8, 14, 16, 20
IV. 4, 6, 7, 9, 18, 20, 25

sourceQuestion:
What are the worst-case complexities of insertion and deletion of a key in a binary search tree?
A. Theta(log n) for both insertion and deletion
B. Theta(n) for both insertion and deletion
C. Theta(log n) for insertion and Theta(n) for deletion
D. Theta(n) for insertion and Theta(log n) for deletion
sourceAnswer: B. Theta(n) for both insertion and deletion

parsed.stem: What are the worst-case complexities of insertion and deletion of a key in a binary search tree?

options.text: Theta(log n) for both insertion and deletion
Theta(n) for both insertion and deletion
Theta(log n) for insertion and Theta(n) for deletion
Theta(n) for insertion and Theta(log n) for deletion

sourceQuestion:
While inserting the elements 71, 65, 84, 69, 67, 83 in an empty binary search tree (BST) in the sequence shown, the element in the lowest level is
A. 65
B. 67
C. 69
D. 83

sourceQuestion:
Let T be a binary search tree with 15 nodes. The minimum and maximum possible heights of T are:
Note: The height of a tree with a single node is 0.
A. 4 and 14 respectively.
B. 3 and 14 respectively.
C. 4 and 15 respectively.
D. 3 and 15 respectively.

sourceQuestion:
In a balanced binary search tree with n elements, what is the worst case time complexity of reporting all elements in range [k1, k2]? Assume that the number of reported elements is k.
A. Theta(log(n))
B. Theta(log(n) + k)
C. Theta(k * log(n))
D. Theta(n)

parsed.stem: In a balanced binary search tree with n elements, what is the worst case time complexity of reporting all elements in range [k1, k2]? Assume that the number of reported elements is k.

sourceQuestion:
The preorder traversal of a binary search tree is 15, 10, 12, 11, 20, 18, 16, 19. Which one of the following is the postorder traversal of the tree?
A. 10, 12, 15, 16, 18, 19, 20, 11
B. 11, 12, 10, 16, 19, 18, 20, 15
C. 10, 11, 12, 20, 19, 18, 16, 15
D. 19, 16, 18, 20, 10, 11, 12, 15

parsed.stem: The preorder traversal of a binary search tree is 15, 10, 12, 11, 20, 18, 16, 19. Which one of the following is the postorder traversal of the tree?

sourceQuestion:
A binary search tree T contains n distinct elements. What is the time complexity of picking an element in T that is smaller than the maximum element in T?
A. Theta(log n)
B. Theta(n)
C. Theta(log n)
D. Theta(1)

parsed.stem: A binary search tree T contains n distinct elements. What is the time complexity of picking an element in T that is smaller than the maximum element in T?

sourceQuestion:
You are given a set S of distinct integers. A binary search tree T is created by inserting all elements of S one by one, starting with an empty tree. The tree T follows the convention that, at each node, all values stored in the left subtree of the node are smaller than the value stored at the node. You are not aware of the sequence in which these values were inserted into T, and you do not have access to S.

Which of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Inorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
B. Root of T can be determined from V.
C. Preorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
D. Postorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
sourceAnswer: A. Inorder traversal of T can be determined from V.

parsed.stem:
You are given a set S of distinct integers. A binary search tree T is created by inserting all elements of S one by one, starting with an empty tree. The tree T follows the convention that, at each node, all values stored in the left subtree of the node are smaller than the value stored at the node. You are not aware of the sequence in which these values were inserted into T, and you do not have access to S.

Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: Inorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
Root of T can be determined from V.
Preorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
Postorder traversal of T can be determined from V.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE for any binary search tree (BST) having n distinct integers?
A. The maximum length of a path from the root node to any other node is n - 1.
B. An inorder traversal will always produce a sorted sequence of elements.
C. Finding an element takes O(log n) time in the worst case.
D. Every BST is a Min-Heap.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE for any binary search tree (BST) having n distinct integers?

options.text: The maximum length of a path from the root node to any other node is n - 1.
An inorder traversal will always produce a sorted sequence of elements.
Finding an element takes O(log n) time in the worst case.

sourceQuestion:

A. p
B. p + 1
C. n - p
D. n - p + 1

A. 2, 1, 4, 3, 6, 7, 8, 5
B. 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 7, 6, 5
C. 2, 1, 4, 3, 8, 7, 6, 5
D. 1, 2, 4, 3, 7, 8, 6, 5

Suppose that we have numbers between 1 and 100 in a binary search tree and want to search for the number 55. Which of the following sequences CANNOT be the sequence of nodes examined?

sourceQuestion:
When searching for the key value 60 in a binary search tree, nodes containing the key values 10, 20, 50, 70, 80, 90 are traversed, not necessarily in the order given. How many different orders are possible in which these key values can occur on the search path from the root to the node containing the value 60?

A. 35
B. 64
C. 128
D. 5040

parsed.stem: When searching for the key value 60 in a binary search tree, nodes containing the key values 10, 20, 50, 70, 80, 90 are traversed, not necessarily in the order given. How many different orders are possible in which these key values can occur on the search path from the root to the node containing the value 60?

sourceQuestion:
A Binary Search Tree (BST) stores values in the range 37 to 573. Consider the following sequences of keys:

I.   81, 537, 102, 439, 285, 376, 305
II.  52, 97, 121, 195, 242, 381, 472
III. 142, 248, 520, 345, 270, 307
IV.  550, 149, 507, 395, 462, 402, 270

Suppose the BST has been unsuccessfully searched for key 273. Which all of the above sequences list nodes in the order in which we could have encountered them in the search?

A. II and III only
B. I and III only
C. III and IV only
D. III only

I.   81, 537, 102, 439, 285, 376, 305

IV.  550, 149, 507, 395, 462, 402, 270

Suppose the BST has been unsuccessfully searched for key 273. Which all of the above sequences list nodes in the order in which we could have encountered them in the search?

sourceQuestion:
A Binary Search Tree (BST) stores values in the range 37 to 573. Consider the following sequences of keys:

I.   81, 537, 102, 439, 285, 376, 305
II.  52, 97, 121, 195, 242, 381, 472

Which of the following statements is TRUE?

A. I and II are inorder sequences of two different BSTs
B. I is a preorder sequence of some BST with 439 as the root
C. II is an inorder sequence of some BST
D. I is a postorder sequence of some BST with 305 as the root
sourceAnswer: A. I and II are inorder sequences of two different BSTs

parsed.stem:
A Binary Search Tree (BST) stores values in the range 37 to 573. Consider the following sequences of keys:

I.   81, 537, 102, 439, 285, 376, 305
II.  52, 97, 121, 195, 242, 381, 472

Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: I and II are inorder sequences of two different BSTs
I is a preorder sequence of some BST with 439 as the root
II is an inorder sequence of some BST
I is a postorder sequence of some BST with 305 as the root
